title: Scaling Django Task Queues With Celery
excerpt: How I structure async jobs, retries, and monitoring for stable background processing.
content:
# Scaling Django Task Queues With Celery

Background jobs look simple in local development, but production traffic can quickly expose retry storms and visibility gaps. This write-up documents a pattern I use to keep queues predictable.

## Queue Design

- Split work by domain, not by random priority labels
- Keep tasks idempotent so safe retry is possible
- Track failures with meaningful context

```python
from celery import shared_task

@shared_task(bind=True, autoretry_for=(Exception,), retry_backoff=True, max_retries=5)
def send_digest_email(self, user_id):
    # Idempotent operation: regenerate payload each execution
    user = User.objects.get(id=user_id)
    payload = build_digest_payload(user)
    email_service.send(user.email, payload)
```

## Operational Notes

The key is observing queue depth, retry rates, and task execution duration as first-class metrics. If any one metric is ignored, incidents become hard to diagnose.

![Task queue dashboard](https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1461749280684-dccba630e2f6?auto=format&fit=crop&w=1200&q=80)

## Result

This setup reduced failed task incidents and made backlog recovery much faster during peak usage windows.

title: Designing Clean DRF APIs For Product Growth
excerpt: A practical API design style that keeps endpoints stable while product requirements evolve.
content:
# Designing Clean DRF APIs For Product Growth

When feature pressure is high, APIs can become inconsistent quickly. I prefer contract-first endpoint design with explicit serializers and a strict error format.

## Serializer-Driven Responses

```python
class ProjectSerializer(serializers.ModelSerializer):
    class Meta:
        model = Project
        fields = ["id", "name", "status", "updated_at"]
```

## Why This Helps

- Frontend teams can rely on stable response shapes
- Validation rules stay close to transport boundaries
- Refactoring internals does not break clients

![API flow design](https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1517180102446-f3ece451e9d8?auto=format&fit=crop&w=1200&q=80)

Consistent API contracts reduce integration friction and speed up releases.

title: Practical FastAPI Patterns For Production
excerpt: Dependency boundaries, request validation, and deployment tips I use in real projects.
content:
# Practical FastAPI Patterns For Production

FastAPI is great for speed, but good structure still matters. Keep your modules explicit and avoid putting business logic directly in route handlers.

## Route Layer

```python
@router.post("/orders", response_model=OrderResponse)
async def create_order(payload: CreateOrderRequest, service: OrderService = Depends(get_order_service)):
    return await service.create(payload)
```

## Service Layer

The service layer owns workflows and invariants. The route layer should only translate HTTP concerns.

![FastAPI architecture](https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1517430816045-df4b7de11d1d?auto=format&fit=crop&w=1200&q=80)

This pattern keeps endpoints testable and makes future changes less risky.
